context.subject: Computer Science - Java Programming

questionList.question:
Java Streams & Collections

Analyze the following Java code snippet and explain what it does:

import java.util.*;
import java.util.stream.*;

public class StreamExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<String> names = Arrays.asList(
            "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "David", "Eve",
            "Frank", "Grace", "Heidi", "Ivan", "Judy"
        );

        Map<Boolean, List<String>> partitioned = names.stream()
            .filter(name -> name.length() > 4)
            .collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(
                name -> name.startsWith("A") || name.startsWith("B") || name.startsWith("C") || name.startsWith("D") || name.startsWith("E") || name.startsWith("F")
            ));

        System.out.println("Matched: " + partitioned.get(true));
        System.out.println("Unmatched: " + partitioned.get(false));
    }
}
questionList.referenceAnswer:
This program demonstrates Java Streams with partitioning:

  • Creates a list of 10 names
  • Filters names with length greater than 4 characters
  • Partitions the filtered names into two groups based on whether they start with letters A-F
  • Prints both groups

Expected output:

Matched: [Alice, Charlie, David, Eve, Frank]
Unmatched: [Grace, Heidi, Ivan, Judy]

questionList.question:
Design Patterns - Observer

Review this Java implementation of the Observer pattern and identify any issues:

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.Map;

interface EventListener {
    void update(String eventType, String message);
}

class EventManager {
    private Map<String, List<EventListener>> listeners = new HashMap<>();

    public void subscribe(String eventType, EventListener listener) {
        listeners
            .computeIfAbsent(eventType, k -> new ArrayList<>())
            .add(listener);
    }

    public void unsubscribe(String eventType, EventListener listener) {
        List<EventListener> users = listeners.get(eventType);
        if (users != null) {
            users.remove(listener);
        }
    }

    public void notify(String eventType, String message) {
        List<EventListener> users = listeners.get(eventType);
        if (users != null) {
            for (EventListener listener : users) {
                listener.update(eventType, message);
            }
        }
    }
}

class EmailAlertListener implements EventListener {
    private String email;

    public EmailAlertListener(String email) {
        this.email = email;
    }

    @Override
    public void update(String eventType, String message) {
        System.out.println(
            "Sending email to " + email
                + " about " + eventType + ": " + message
        );
    }
}

class SmsAlertListener implements EventListener {
    private String phoneNumber;

    public SmsAlertListener(String phoneNumber) {
        this.phoneNumber = phoneNumber;
    }

    @Override
    public void update(String eventType, String message) {
        System.out.println(
            "Sending SMS to " + phoneNumber
                + " about " + eventType + ": " + message
        );
    }
}
questionList.referenceAnswer:
The Observer pattern implementation has the following issues:

  • Thread safety: The HashMap and ArrayList are not thread-safe. Use ConcurrentHashMap and CopyOnWriteArrayList for concurrent access
  • Memory leaks: Listeners are never automatically removed. Consider using WeakReferences
  • Null safety: The notify method doesn't handle null eventType gracefully
  • Missing hashCode/equals: EventListener implementations don't override these methods, making unsubscribe unreliable

questionList.question:
Java Generics

What is the output of this code?

import java.util.*;

public class GenericsTest {
    public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T findMax(List<T> list) {
        if (list == null || list.isEmpty()) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException(
                "List cannot be null or empty"
            );
        }

        T max = list.get(0);
        for (T item : list) {
            if (item.compareTo(max) > 0) {
                max = item;
            }
        }
        return max;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(
            3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3, 5, 8,
            9, 7, 9, 3, 2, 3, 8, 4, 6, 2, 6, 4,
            3, 3, 8, 3, 2, 7, 9, 5, 0, 2, 8, 8,
            4, 1, 9, 7
        );
        System.out.println("Max number: " + findMax(numbers));

        List<String> words = Arrays.asList(
            "apple", "zebra", "banana", "mango",
            "cherry", "elderberry", "dragonfruit"
        );
        System.out.println("Max word: " + findMax(words));
    }
}
questionList.referenceAnswer:
The output is:

Max number: 9
Max word: zebra

The findMax method uses bounded type parameters (T extends Comparable<T>) to find the maximum element in a list. For integers, it returns 9. For strings, it returns "zebra" because String comparison is lexicographic (alphabetical order).

questionList.question:
Concurrency - Thread Pool

Explain what this concurrent program does and identify potential issues:

import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicInteger;
import java.util.List;
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class ThreadPoolDemo {
    private static final AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger(0);

    public static void main(String[] args)
            throws InterruptedException, ExecutionException {
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);
        List<Future<Integer>> futures = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 0; i < 20; i++) {
            final int taskId = i;
            futures.add(executor.submit(() -> {
                System.out.println(
                    Thread.currentThread().getName()
                        + " processing task " + taskId
                );
                Thread.sleep(100 + (long) (Math.random() * 200));
                return counter.incrementAndGet();
            }));
        }

        int total = 0;
        for (Future<Integer> future : futures) {
            total += future.get();
        }
        System.out.println("Total processed: " + total);

        executor.shutdown();
        if (!executor.awaitTermination(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
            executor.shutdownNow();
        }
    }
}
questionList.referenceAnswer:
This program demonstrates:

  • Creates a fixed thread pool with 4 threads
  • Submits 20 tasks that simulate work with random sleep times
  • Each task increments an atomic counter and returns the new value
  • Main thread collects all results and sums them
  • Gracefully shuts down the executor

Potential issues:

  • The sum of returned values may not equal 20*21/2 due to race conditions in how results are collected
  • No timeout on individual future.get() calls could cause indefinite blocking
  • Exception handling in tasks is swallowed - should use Callable with proper error handling

questionList.question:
Java Records & Pattern Matching

What does this Java 17+ code output?

sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}

record Circle(double radius) implements Shape {}
record Rectangle(double width, double height) implements Shape {}
record Triangle(double base, double height) implements Shape {}

public class PatternMatchingDemo {
    static String describe(Shape shape) {
        return switch (shape) {
            case Circle c -> String.format(
                "Circle with radius %.2f, area=%.2f, circumference=%.2f",
                c.radius,
                Math.PI * c.radius * c.radius,
                2 * Math.PI * c.radius
            );
            case Rectangle r -> String.format(
                "Rectangle %.2fx%.2f, area=%.2f, perimeter=%.2f",
                r.width(), r.height(),
                r.width() * r.height(),
                2 * (r.width() + r.height())
            );
            case Triangle t -> String.format(
                "Triangle base=%.2f height=%.2f, area=%.2f",
                t.base(), t.height(),
                0.5 * t.base() * t.height()
            );
        };
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape[] shapes = {
            new Circle(5),
            new Rectangle(4, 6),
            new Triangle(3, 8)
        };
        for (Shape s : shapes) {
            System.out.println(describe(s));
        }
    }
}
questionList.referenceAnswer:
Output:

Circle with radius 5.00, area=78.54, circumference=31.42
Rectangle 4.00x6.00, area=24.00, perimeter=20.00
Triangle base=3.00 height=8.00, area=12.00

This demonstrates Java 17+ features:

  • Sealed interfaces: Shape only permits three implementations
  • Records: Immutable data classes with auto-generated getters, equals, hashCode, toString
  • Pattern matching in switch: Type patterns with binding variables
  • Arrow syntax: No fall-through, no break needed

items.submittedAnswer:
This code creates a list of names and uses Java Streams to partition them. First it filters names longer than 4 characters, then separates them into two groups based on whether they start with letters A through F.

Matched: [Alice, Charlie, David, Eve, Frank]
Unmatched: [Grace, Heidi, Ivan, Judy]
I think it's filtering and sorting names somehow using streams. Not sure about the partitioning part.
The Observer pattern looks correct. EventManager handles subscriptions and notifications. EmailAlertListener and SmsAlertListener implement the EventListener interface.

Issues I found:

  • Not thread-safe - HashMap should be ConcurrentHashMap
  • Potential memory leaks if listeners aren't removed
  • No null checks in notify method
  • EventListener implementations should override equals/hashCode for proper unsubscribe behavior

The output is:

Max number: 9
Max word: zebra

The findMax method finds the largest element using compareTo. For numbers it's 9, for strings it's "zebra" (last alphabetically).
This creates a thread pool with 4 threads and submits 20 tasks. Each task sleeps for a random time and increments an atomic counter. The main thread waits for all results and prints the total.
It's some kind of multithreading example with futures and executors. I'm not sure about the details.

This uses Java records and sealed interfaces with pattern matching switch expressions. The output calculates area and perimeter for different shapes.

Circle with radius 5.00, area=78.54, circumference=31.42
Rectangle 4.00x6.00, area=24.00, perimeter=20.00
Triangle base=3.00 height=8.00, area=12.00
